coredata.dc:title: Okra peel-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots: Eco-friendly synthesis and multi-functional applications in heavy metal ion sensing, nitro compound detection and environmental remediation

coredata.dc:description: The present study explores the kitchen waste okra peels derived synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) via simple carbonization followed by reflux method. The synthesized N-CDs was characterized using, TEM, XPS, FTIR, XRD, Raman, UV–Visible and Fluorescence Spectroscopy. The N-CDs emits bright blue emission at 420 nm with 12 % of quantum yield as well as it follows excitation dependent emission. Further, the N-CDs were employed as a fluorescence sensor for detection of hazardous metal ions and nitro compounds. Among various metal ions and nitro compounds, the N-CDs shows fluorescence quenching response towards Cr6+, and Mn7+ metal ions as well as 4-nitroaniline (4-NA) and picric acid (PA) with significant hypsochromic and bathochromic shift for Mn7+, 4-NA and PA respectively. The developed fluorescent probe shows relatively low limit of detection (LOD) of 1.46 µg/mL, 1.05 µg/mL, 2.1 µg/mL and 2.2 µg/mL for the above analytes respectively. The N-CDs did not show any significant interference with coexisting ions and successfully applied for real water sample analysis. In addition, circular economy approach was employed for adsorption of dyes by reactivating leftover waste carbon residue which was obtained after reflux. Thus, the kitchen waste valorization and circular economy approach based N-CDs have potential applications in the field of detection of emerging pollutants, and environmental remediation.

head.ce:title: Okra peel-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots: Eco-friendly synthesis and multi-functional applications in heavy metal ion sensing, nitro compound detection and environmental remediation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study explores the kitchen waste okra peels derived synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) via simple carbonization followed by reflux method. The synthesized N-CDs was characterized using, TEM, XPS, FTIR, XRD, Raman, UV–Visible and Fluorescence Spectroscopy. The N-CDs emits bright blue emission at 420 nm with 12 % of quantum yield as well as it follows excitation dependent emission. Further, the N-CDs were employed as a fluorescence sensor for detection of hazardous metal ions and nitro compounds. Among various metal ions and nitro compounds, the N-CDs shows fluorescence quenching response towards Cr6+, and Mn7+ metal ions as well as 4-nitroaniline (4-NA) and picric acid (PA) with significant hypsochromic and bathochromic shift for Mn7+, 4-NA and PA respectively. The developed fluorescent probe shows relatively low limit of detection (LOD) of 1.46 µg/mL, 1.05 µg/mL, 2.1 µg/mL and 2.2 µg/mL for the above analytes respectively. The N-CDs did not show any significant interference with coexisting ions and successfully applied for real water sample analysis. In addition, circular economy approach was employed for adsorption of dyes by reactivating leftover waste carbon residue which was obtained after reflux. Thus, the kitchen waste valorization and circular economy approach based N-CDs have potential applications in the field of detection of emerging pollutants, and environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is the basic and essential need of every living organism, however discharge of organic and inorganic toxic contaminants into fresh water leads to water pollution. Worldwide the water pollution is one of the great challenges to human beings and other living organisms. The global industrialization and various human activities are the prime cause of the contamination of pollutants into the water bodies, which has several hazardous effects to the ecosystem and ultimately to the human health. Wastewater from textile industries especially dying and printing factories are considered to be one of the major sources of water pollution [1]. Also, the nitro compounds such as 4-nitroaniline (4-NA) and picric acid (PA) have been used with great extent for the manufacturing of various dyes, pesticides, pharmaceuticals, and explosive materials and these are the key components of pollution in various water bodies [2]. The organic and inorganic contaminants such as heavy metals, dyes, and nitro compounds are directly released into potable water sources without prior pretreatments by textiles industries, mining, electroplating, electronic manufacturing, and oil refining, etc. [3–6]. These emerging pollutants have greatest hazard to environment and living beings even at trace level. The excess amount of heavy metals like chromium, manganese and nitro compound like 4-NA, and PA contaminates the water sources and eventually enter into ecosystem of aquatic animals and human beings which greatly responsible for serious diseases like skin sensitization, headache, liver damage and anemia, etc [7–9]. Thus, the water pollution is global challegnge for countries all across the globe.

There are several methods have been developed for detection of heavy metals [10] and nitro compounds in drinking water as well as in soil by using analytical technique such as Atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), Inductive Coupled Plasma-Mass Spectroscopy/ Optical Emission Spectroscopy (ICP-MS/OES), Atomic Fluorescence Spectrometry (AFS), Laser Induced–Breakdown Spectrometry, X-ray Fluorescence Spectrometry, Cyclic Voltammetry, etc. but, these are costly and time consuming techniques [11–16]. Nowadays, the metal organic framework (MOF) and semiconducting quantum dots based methods have been used for the sensing of hazardous pollutants. These methods are effective for sensing of heavy metals, organic and inorganic pollutant in water. However, due to high cost, use of toxic chemicals and complex synthesis of semiconducting quantum dots and MOF limits their applicability [15,17,18]. Thus, there is need to develop quick, sensitive, low-cost and sustainable sensing method for detection of the emerging pollutants in water bodies at trace level.

Therefore, the carbon dots (CDs) based sensing methods received great attention for detection of various analytes, biomolecules, pharmaceuticals, personal care products as well as emerging pollutants in water samples. The CDs possess biocompatible and photo-physical properties such as, low toxicity, photoluminescence properties, ease of surface functionalisation, facile synthesis, excellent photo-stability and water solubility [19,20]. In addition, the outstanding properties of CDs make them applicable in various fields such as, in vitro and in vivo bio-imaging, photo-catalysis, anti-counterfeiting, drug delivery, light emitting diodes (LED) and fluorescence sensing, etc. [3,21–25]. Due to fluorescence emission properties and ease of surface functionalization, CDs possess the ability to selective and sensitive detection of analytes, which make them rising star for fluorescence based sensor for detection of analyte pollutant from water bodies. There are several methods have been developed for synthesis of (N-CDs) generally recognized as “top down” and “bottom up” methods [26]. The bottom up approach is more accessible as compared to top down route and acts as a cost effective and facile and thus the researchers preferred bottom up route for synthesis of CDs. Generally, CDs are synthesized by hydrothermal, microwave assisted, pyrolysis, chemical oxidation, etc. methods [27–29]. Further, to enhance the surface functionalization as well as optical properties the reports were available on the synthesis of heteroatom (Nitrogen, Sulphur, Zinc, Phosphorus, Magnesium, etc.) doped CDs [30–34]. In literature, the chemical precursors such as, citric acid, phenylenediamine, ammonium citrate, unsaturated amides, cysteine, etc.[11,35] were used for the synthesis of CDs but, these chemical precursors are more costly and some are toxic in nature. The Xiaochun Zheng et al. synthesized carbon dots from carbon nano sheets and successfully employ for sensing of Cr6+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ having LOD of 242, 251 and 161, nM respectively[36]. The research group of Lei Cai have developed polymer based graphene quantum dots based fluorescent sensing technique for detection of 4-nitro aniline having LOD of 7.0 nM [37]. Whereas, in some reports CDs were used for sensing of metal ion as well as nitro compounds simultaneously. The Zong Siyu and co-researchers have developed coffee residue derived carbon dots for fluorescence sensing of Fe2+ and picric acid [38]. However, in our work kitchen waste valorized nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were used for multifunctional application and used for detection of metal ions such as Cr6+, Mn7+ as well as for nitro compounds such as 4-Nitroaniline and picric acid. In Table 1 various biomass and biowaste precursors derived nitrogen doped carbon dots for sensing of metal ions and nitro compound have been compared with current data. (SEE Table 2.
Table 3. ).

Thus, now research is moving towards the use of sustainable as well as biomass and bio-waste derived synthesis of CDs; because the biomass is a good natural source of carbon as well as it easily available and cost effective. There are several biomass precursors have been used for synthesis of CDs for detection of various pollutants [7,33,39,40]. In literature reports and best of our knowledge there is no single report on waste okra peel derived synthesis of CDs. The waste okra peels is very common kitchen waste in Indian household with high percentage of carbohydrate (cellulose, hemicellulose) and lignin which make them a good source of raw carbon [41]. By keeping sustainable, cost-effective and eco-synthesis approach, we have synthesized N-CDs from kitchen derived waste okra peels.

Thus, in the present study the kitchen waste (okra peel) valorization approach was employed for synthesis of highly fluorescent N-CDs via simple carbonization followed by reflux method. Further, the synthesized N-CDs were characterized structurally and optically for understanding of surface functionality, size as well as the impact of dopant on fluorescence of N-CDs by different characterisation techniques. The synthesized N-CDs were employed for detection of metal ions and nitro compounds based on fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Cr6+, Mn7+, 4-nitroaniline, and picric acid. Moreover, circular economy approach was employed with leftover waste carbon residue obtained from reflux method was further reactivated and used for adsorption of model pollutant dyes with approximate 90 % adsorption capacity. The successfully developed fluorescent probe and left-over residual carbon having potential applications in the field of sensing and detection of emerging pollutants as well as environmental remediation.

Waste Biomass (Okra peels) was collected from kitchen in bulk, then dried naturally and stored for further use. All nitro compounds used in the experiment were of analytical grade and used without further purification. All the metal salts and dye chemicals NiCl2, CoCl2, NaCl, ZnCl2, CaCl2, HgCl2, SnCl2, MgCl2, K2Cr2O7, PbCl2, KMnO4, LiCl2, FeCl3, AuCl3, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, 4-nitroaniline, 4-nitrobenzaldehyade, 3-nitroacetophenon, picric acid, 3-nitroaniline, m-dinitrobenzene, 2,4-dinitrotolune, 2-nitrophenol, 2- nitroaniline, 3- nitroaniline, nitrobenzene, methylene blue, crystal violet, rhodamine B, safranin orange and victoria blue, etc. were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories (SRL), PVT. LTD. (Mumbai, India.). All required stock solutions of 100 µg/mL were prepared in distilled water (D.W.).

Fluorescence of N-CDs were measured using a spectrofluorometer (JASCO, FP-8300, Japan) with a 5 nm band pass and a xenon arc lamp as a source. UV–Visible spectra of N-CDs were recorded using the UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Specord-210 Plus Analytikjena, Germany) with a 1.0 cm quartz cell. Surface morphology, shape as well as size and crystallinity of N-CDs were measured using transmittance electron microscopy (TEM) (PHILIPS CM200) with an operating voltage of 200 kV and an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Bruker D8 Advance, Germany) with CuKα radiation having 1.5406 A◦ wavelength, respectively. The elemental composition of N-CDs was analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (JEOL, JPS-9030, Japan). The surface functionalization of N-CDs was studied by Fourier Transformed Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) using (Bruker ALPHA, Germany). The confocal Raman spectrometer was used for Raman spectroscopy (Renishow, UK).

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized from kitchen waste okra peel as a carbon precursor using a simple carbonization method followed by reflux method as shown in Fig. 1 . The dry okra peels were heated in a muffle furnace at 350 °C temperature for 210 min to obtain carbon black. Further, N-CDs were synthesized as per previous report with slight modification [42]. In brief, 2 gm of carbon black was dispersed in 200 mL of 0.1 M HNO3 and refluxed it for 12 h in round bottom flask. The resultant pale-yellow solution indicated the formation of N-CDs. The obtained solution was filtrated through Whatman filter paper No. 41 and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min for removal of residual carbon and impurities. Then, the resultant filtrate is passed through 0.2 µm syringe filter membrane for elimination of larger particles and the obtained N-CDs solution was stored at 4°C and used for further experiment.

The waste carbon residue obtained after reflux was collected and further reactivated by simple chemical activation method. In brief, for reactivation the carbon residue was soaked in 1 M KOH for 24 h and dried at 100 ℃. Then, the dried carbon was heated in muffle furnace at 350 ℃ for 3 h. After that, the obtained reactivated carbon powder was washed with D.W. to remove impurities, dried, and stored for further experiment.

The stock solution (100 µg/mL) of different metal ions such as (Ni2+, Co2+, Na+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Sn2+, Mg2+, Cr6+, Pb2+, Mn7+, Li+, Fe3+, Au3+, Al3+) were prepared. The 0.5 mL of each metal ion solution was added in separate test tube containing 0.5 mL of N-CDs and diluted to 5 mL in volumetric flask using D.W. The obtained solution was kept at room temperature for 10 min and fluorescence emission spectra was recorded at 330 nm excitation wavelength.

The similar process discussed for metal ion sensing was followed for sensing of nitro-compound. In brief, the 100 µg/mL stock solutions of 4-nitroaniline, 4-nitrobenzaldehyade, 3-nitroacetophenon, picric acid, 3-nitroaniline, m-dinitrobenzene, 2,4-dinitrotolune, 2-nitrophenol were prepared. Then, the 0.5 mL solution was added in 0.5 mL of N-CDs and volume was maintained to 5 mL. Further, the solutions was kept at room temperature for 10 min and fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at 330 nm excitation wavelength.

The cytotoxicity test was performed on MCF-7 human breast cancer cell line. The MCF-7 human breast cancer cell line was obtained from National Center for Cell Science (NCCS), Pune and made ready for cytotoxicity test as given in previous repot [43]. The MTT (3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5 diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) assay of N-CDs was carried out on MCF-7 cell. In brief, the cells were seeded on 96 well plate and incubated at 37 ˚C with concentration 1 X 106 cells/mL containing 5 % of CO2. After 24 h, 10 µL MTT reagent and different concentration of N-CDs from 10 to 50 µL in 1 mL of PBS added in cell culture media and incubated for 1 h at 37 ˚C in 5 % CO2 environment. Further, the cell was imagining under fluorescence microscope 20 X magnification. The cell viability was calculated by using equation (1).

The optical properties of N-CDs were analyzed using UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. In UV–visible spectroscopy, the absorbance of N-CDs at 294 nm indicated π-π* transition because of sp2 hybridization of the C = C or C = O group, which is due to the formation of carbon in the graphitic core (Fig. 2 a) [44,45].The main feature of N-CDs is their highly intense fluorescence spectra, which have an emission wavelength of 420 nm while being excited at 330 nm (Fig. 2a). The quantum yield (QY) of synthesized N-CDs was calculated by the comparative method using equation S1 and taking quinine sulfate as a reference, which was found to be 12 %. Further, the excitation-dependent emission behavior of N-CDs was studied by varying the wavelength from 300 nm to 390 nm (Fig. 2b). It was observed that the emission wavelength shows red shift with increasing excitation wavelength with the highest emission centered at 420 nm while excited at 330 nm. The non-uniformity in particle size and the availability of phonon state dominated the electronic transition in band which leads to excitation dependent emission behavior of N-CDs [46,47].

The stability of N-CDs in pH as well as in salt solution was studied. To understand the stability of N-CDs in a pH solution, pH values from 2 to 12 were subjected, as shown in Fig. S2a. N-CDs was quite stable from pH 5 to 11, while in acidic condition (<3) protonation of amino groups and carboxyl groups leads to aggregation of N-CDs, resulting slight quenching in fluorescence intensity of N-CDs in highly acidic condition. Although, the fluorescence intensity is slightly enhanced at pH 12 due to deprotonation of carboxyl and amino group present on surface of N-CDs [48–51]. Also, the effect of ionic strength was studied using an increasing concentration of NaCl, there was no significant effect of ionic strength on fluorescence intensity or emission wavelength of N-CDs was observed (Fig. S2b). These results shows that the N-CDs are quite stable in salt and pH solution that make them potential candidates for fluorescence sensing applications at all pH ranges (pH 2 to 12). The photo stability of N-CDs (10 µg/mL) was evaluated by fluorescence spectrophotometer, where fluorescence decay was measured at 330 nm with increasing time from 5 to 600 min. Further, the N-CDs was stored at −4 ˚C and fluorescence spectra was recorded for 10 days. The Fig. S2c indicated the 7 % loss in fluorescence intensity and Fig. S2d shows 14 % fluorescence intensity of N-CDs decreases over the 10 days of time interval. These obtained result shows that, the N-CDs have good photo stability. The effect of polar and non-polar solvent on solubility of N-CDs was investigated as shown in Fig. S2e. Herein, to check the solvent effect on fluorescence of N-CDs, the polar solvents (water, DMSO, acetonitrile, acetone, methanol, isopropanol, formaldehyde, ethanol, acetic acid, ethyl acetate, and glycerol) and non-polar solvents (chloroform, eenzene, toluene, xylene, and cyclohexane) were used. Interestingly, the results indicated that the N-CDs were more soluble in polar protic solvents as compared to polar aprotic and non-polar solvents. From Fig. S2e, it shows that water is more stable solvent for N-CDs while fluorescence intensity is high in polar solvents due to inter- and intra-molecular hydrogen bonding [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology and functionalization of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology, particle size and shape of N-CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopic (TEM) analysis (Fig. 3 ). The Fig. 3a shows the quasi-spherical shape of N-CDs with inter-planar lattice spacing (d-spacing) of 0.27 nm, which well matches with the XRD planes (Fig. S3a). The pattern of selected area electron diffraction (SAED) determines that N-CDs have a graphitic nature as shown in Fig. 3b.The histogram in Fig. 3cshows particle size distribution of N-CDs with particle size ranges from 2 to 7 nm with the average particle size of 4 nm.

The elemental composition of N-CDs was analyzed through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The high-resolution XPS spectra in Fig. 4 a indicates the presence of C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks around 285.07, 400.07, and 531.07 eV, respectively. The deconvolution of the C1s core spectrum contributed to three peaks at 284.9, 285.8, and 289.01 eV, indicating carbon was present in C–C, C = C, and C-N forms, as shown in Fig. 4b. Further, the deconvolution spectra of N1s indicated in Fig. 4c. contributed to three peaks, which are 399.7, 400.3, and 403.9 eV, respectively, indicating that nitrogen was present in the form of N–H, C-N, and C = N, respectively. While, the two high resolution deconvoluted peaks of oxygen at 531.4 and 533.06 eV indicated the formation of C-O/C = O and C-OH bonds respectively, as shown in Fig. 4d [52]. The presence of N1s peak confirms the successful doping of nitrogen element into carbon core.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of N-CDs was shown in Fig. S3a, determines the amorphous nature of N-CDs, the sharp peck at 2θ = 14° and 19° are due to the (100) and (002) planes, respectively, which indicates the graphitic nature of carbon dots [53–55]. While, the sharp peak at 2θ = 27° indicates successful doping of nitrogen in carbon dots [44]. The surface functional group of N-CDs was analyzed by FTIR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. S3b. The broad IR spectra at 3321 cm−1 is due to stretching vibrations of the –OH bond [42]. The presence of short peak at 3739 cm−1 corresponds to the presence of -N–H stretching vibrations. The peak at1639 cm−1 shows unsaturated double bond stretching of the C = C or C = O bond, 2924 and 2860 cm−1 peaks are due to methylene and methyl C–H stretching, respectively. The peak at 2134 cm−1 is due to C≡C stretching and the small peak at 1378 cm−1 is due to an alkyne C–H bend. The vibration band at 1044 cm−1 with N-CDs corresponded to the primary alcohol C-O bond [56].

The Raman spectra of N-CDs in Fig. S4 gives two bands at 1336 and 1556 cm−1 represented D (Distorted nature) shows sp3 hybridization of carbon and G (Graphitic nature) sp2 hybridization of carbon of N-CDs, respectively. The ratio of relative intensity of D and G band (ID/IG) gives the information regarding to degree of graphitization of carbon. The value of intensity ratio < 1 indicated synthesized carbon material is more graphitized [50,57,58]. Herein, the ID/IG ratio for N-CDs was measured to be 0.6 indicating well graphitized carbon structure of N-CDs which also matches with XRD and TEM results of N-CDs.

The selectivity experiment was carried out with 15 different metal ions, and among them, N-CDs showed fluorescence quenching for Cr6+ and Mn7+ only, as shown in Fig. 5 a. Interestingly, Mn7+ shows fluorescence quenching of N-CDs with a slight blue shift of 15 nm and shows instant decolonization. Whereas, the Cr6+ quenches fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with constant wavelength. The Cr6+ and Mn7+ metal ions quenches more than 50 % of fluorescence intensity of N-CDs (Fig. 5b). While, other metal ions do not show any significant change in florescence intensity, from this observation, it shows that N-CDs have a good fluorescence sensitivity towards Mn7+ and Cr6+ metal ions. Further, linearity studies for Mn7+ and Cr6+ were carried out for the calculation of the limit of detection and to understand the sensitivity of N-CDs towards Mn7+ and Cr6+ metal ions.

As increasing concentration of Mn7+ and Cr6+ fluorescence intensity of N-CDs gradually decreases. The concentration of Mn7+ was increased from 2 to 20 µg/mL in known volume of N-CDs represented in Fig. 6 a with decreasing fluorescence intensity of N-CDs + Mn7+ shows hypsochromic shift (blue shift) with 15 nm difference. While, for Cr6+ metal ion concentration of N-CDs + Cr6+ solution was varied from 2 to 30 µg/mL as shown in Fig. 6c. In this case, Cr6+ linearly quenches fluorescence intensity without any shift in emission wavelength. The quenching and shifting of emission wavelength of N-CDs in presence of Mn7+ and Cr6+ metal ions which will be discussed latter in quenching mechanism. The linear relation of Mn7+ and Cr6+ with N-CDs was also examined by UV–Vis absorbance spectra (Fig. S5). The absorbance of Mn7+ and Cr6+ in presence of N-CDs increases with increasing concentration shows good linear response of metal ions to N-CDs. While, in case of Mn7+ the vanishing of characteristic absorption peak of Mn7+ in presence of N-CDs indicating reduction process. The Stern-Volmer plot in Fig. 6b and Fig. 6d was determination by using below equation (2).
(2)
Fo
F
= 1 + K s v
Q
= 1 + K q τ o
[ Q ]

Where, F and F0 are fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with and without addition of metal ions respectively, [Q] is the concentration of metal ion (quencher), Ksv is Stern-Volmer constant, Kq is quenching rate constant and τ0 is relative life time. Here, F0/F plotted against [Q] represents the linear relationship between N-CDs with concentration of Mn7+ and Cr6+ metal ions respectively, having R2 value of 0.99. This Stern-Volmer plot used for calculation of detection limit using equation of LOD = 3.3*σ/Ksv were σ is standard deviation and Stern-Volmer constant Ksv is slope of linear plot. The N-CDs shown good linear response towards Cr6+ and Mn7+ metal ions with linear range of 0 to 30 µg/mL and 0 to 20 µg/mL, respectively. It is found that limit of detection for Mn7+ is 1.051 µg/mL and for Cr6+ it is 1.466 µg/mL.

The life time measurement alone cannot describe the whole quenching mechanism, further to confirm the quenching mechanism of N-CDs with metal ion, UV–Vis absorbance spectra of N-CDs with metal ion was examined as shown in Fig. 7c. The absorbance of Mn7+ in presence of N-CDs clearly shows reduction of Mn7+ to Mn4+ by disapearing of characteristic peak of 506 nm, 526 nm, 546 nm and 568 nm. This result indicated that there is formation of complex of Mn7+ with N-CDs at ground level and reduce to form brown precipitate. While, in case of Cr6+ the UV–Vis absorbance spectra at 262 nm vanishes and peak at 362 nm shifted in presence of N-CDs indicating ground state complex formation between Cr6+ and N-CDs [60,61]. Further, in Fig. 7d the UV–Vis absorbance spectra of Cr6+ was overlapping with the excitation spectra of N-CDs which creates the possibility of quenching was due to primary inner filter effect (IFE). There is high possibility of Cr6+ absorbed excitation radiation and gives turn off mechanism [62]. The IR spectra of N-CDs in presence of Mn7+ and Cr6+ shown in Fig. S6a. The remarkable change in % transmittance at 3303 cm−1 and shifting of 2134 cm−1 to 2090 cm−1 after Mn7+ and Cr6+ addition suggested that strong complex formation between Mn7+ and Cr6+ with N-CDs [63]. The visible decolourization and formation of brown precipitate of Mn7+ ion in presence of N-CDs as shown in Fig. S6b determines the redox reaction between unsaturated carbon group present on N-CDs and Mn7+. The N-CDs are rich in C = C as shown in XPS and IR, it likely to interact with Mn7+ ion which in the form of MnO4¯ in aqueous condition and undergoes redox reaction and forms MnO2. The alkene group in N-CDs reacted with MnO4¯ and reduce to MnO2, which is responsible for ground state complex formation and florescence quenching [60,64]. The results indicating that there was ground state complex formation between N-CDs and Mn7+ and Cr6+ as well as IFE also plays pivotal role in fluorescence quenching in case of Cr6+ metal ion.

To investigate the effect of other metal ions on quenching of Mn7+ and Cr6+, different metal ions (Ni2+, Co2+, Na+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Sn2+, Mg2+, Cr6+, Pb2+, Mn7+, Li+, Fe3+, Au3+, Al3+) in 10 µg/mL concentration added into 10 µg/mL solution of N-CDs + Mn7+ and N-CDs + Cr6+. The Fig. 9 a and 9b resulting that there was no any significant effect of presence of other metal ions on florescence quenching except Mn7+ and Cr6+.

The N-CDs was further utilized as nitro compound sensor. The noticeable fluorescence quenching response towards 4-nitroaniline (4-NA) and picric acid (PA) was observed. The nitro compounds such as 4-nitroaniline (4-NA), 4-nitrobenzaldehyade, 3-nitroacetophenon, picric acid (PA), 3-nitroaniline, m-dinitrobenzene, 2,4-dinitrotolune, 2-nitrophenol were used for the selectivity study. Interestingly, N-CDs shows the fluorescence quenching for 4-NA and PA with significant red shift in wavelength as shown in Fig. 10 a. The 4-NA and PA shows about 50 % of fluorescence quenching as compared to other nitro compounds as shown in Fig. 10b. The study of effect of increasing concentration of 4-NA and PA was carried out by varied concentration from 4 to 30 µg/mL as shown in Fig. 10c and 10e. The results indicate that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs gradually decreases with increasing concentration of 4-NA and PA with constant bathochromic shift in wavelength of about 37 nm and 38 nm for 4-NA and PA respectively. The reason behind bathochromic shift was due to the presence of nitro group on para position which is further explained in following discussion. The Stern-Volmer plot describing linear behavior of increasing concentration of 4-NA and PA. The R2 value of regression line is ∼ 0.99 for 4- NA and PA was given in Fig. 10d, f. With the help of Stern - Volmer plot. The lowest detection limit for 4-NA and PA were calculated which was found to be 2.1 µg/mL and 2.2 µg/mL respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing mechanism for nitro compounds
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching mechanism between N-CDs and nitro compound was initially explored by time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) experiment. The experiment was followed same as for metal ions. The fluorescence life time for 4-NAwas measured to be 437.36 ps, 357.84 ps, and 477.12 ps shown in Fig. 11 a. Similarly, for picric acid fluorescence life time was 477.12 ps, 477.12 ps, 477.12 ps, as shown in Fig. 11b. The fluorescence life time decay value gives τ0/ τ = ∼1 suggesting static type of quenching [65]. The fluorescence life time decay alone couldn’t give a clear version of fluorescence quenching mechanism of analyte. To get the better view of quenching mechanism of 4-NA and PA optical studies were performed as shown in Fig. 11c. In case of 4–NA an absorbance spectra overlaps on emission spectra of N-CDs suggesting secondary IFE. While, the absorbance spectra of PA overlapping the excitation spectra of N-CDs suggesting the primary IFE [62]. Inner Filter Effect (IFE) is important property of analyte in spectroscopy model for non-radiative energy conversion. The primary inner filter effect was occurred due to absorption of excitation radiation by PA, while, the secondary inner filter effect was occurred due to the absorption of emission radiation by 4-NA [62,66]. This result suggested that static quenching and IFE was solely responsible for quenching of 4-NA and PA.

The electron deficiency in nitro group possibly forms the strong complex with functional groups of N-CDs. Further, for better understanding of effect of position of nitro group on quenching of 4-NA and PA, the compound which containing different position of nitro group and without nitro group were studied as shown in Fig. 11d. The nitro compound such as 2-nitroaniline (2-NA), 3-nitroaniline (3-NA), 4-nitroaniline (4-NA), nitrobenzene and picric acid shows fluorescence quenching of 20.71 %, 2.0 %, 42.5 %, 13.3% and 51.7 % respectively while, aniline don’t show quenching at all, which clearly confirming that presence of nitro group on para position was responsible of quenching, the strong π-π interaction of graphitic carbon core and the π cloud of nitro groups on para position was a major driving force which bring them together and allows to form complex with surface functional group present on N-CDs [2,5,30].

The sensing capacity of metal ions (Mn7+ and Cr6+) and nitro compounds (4-NA and PA) was evaluated in environmental water samples, using Panchganga river and Shivaji University lake water samples. The test was carried out by heating and filtering the river and lake water samples to remove dust and impurities before using for experiment. The known amount of metal ions and nitro compounds were spiked into water samples and fluorescence spectra was recorded. The Table No. 2 represents the obtained experimental results which shows that the developed N-CDs based fluorescence probe having very good relative standard deviation (RSD) and recovery rate between 88 and 98 %. These results indicate that the N-CDs as fluorescence sensor can be applicable for real water sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of waste carbon residue for dye removal
ce:section.ce:para:
The circular economy approach based leftover waste carbon residue was reactivated by chemical activation method and applied for adsorption of various dyes by batch adsorption experiment. The 10 mg of reactivated carbon (RAC) was added into 10 ppm of cationic dyes which effectively adsorbed the different dyes such as Methylene blue (MB), Crystal violet (CV), Rhodamine B (RHB), Safranin orange (SO), Victoria blue (VB) as shows in Fig. 12 . The Table No. 3 represents the % Removal efficiency of dye which was calculated by using equation (3) and (4) respectively.
(3)

R e m o v a l =

Where, Qe (mg/L) is maximum adsorption efficiency, Co (mg/L) is initial concentration of dye, Ce (mg/L) is the equilibrium concentration of dye, V (L) is volume of solution and m (mg) is the mass of the activated carbon.
The RAC from N-CDs carbon residue shown good adsorption capacity for cationic model pollutant dyes.

The cell viability effect of N-CDs was evaluated using MTT assay on the MCF-7 human breast cancer cell line. The different concentrations of N-CDs (10 to 50 µL) was added into MCF-7 cell line and incubated for 24 h. The cell viability with increasing concentration of N-CDs was shown in Fig. S7. The result indicated around 49.5 % reduction on cell viability with increasing concentration of N-CDs as compared to control. The synthesized N-CDs induce inhibitor activity against MCF-7 cell line which indicated N-CDs have anti cancerous activity [43,50,67].

The waste okra peel derived N-CDs was successfully synthesized through simple carbonization followed by reflux method using HNO3 as a nitrogen source. The optimized N-CDs was characterized through TEM, XPS, XRD, IR and Raman spectroscopy for their physicochemical properties. It is thoroughly proving that the N-CDs have around 4 nm particle size with graphitic nature as well as C = O, C = N, C = C and –OH functional groups decorated on the surface of N-CDs. The surface functionalization helps N-CDs for selective and sensitive detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ metal ions as well as 4-NA and PA nitro compounds. The N-CDs senses Cr6+ and Mn7+ with detection limit of 1.051 µg/mL and 1.466 (∼1.5) µg/mL, respectively as well as for 4-NA and PA detection limit was 2.1 µg/mL and 2.2 µg/mL, respectively. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was appearing due to ground state complex formation which leads to static quenching mechanism as well as IFE also plays significant role in fluorescence quenching of Cr6+, 4-NA and PA. Additionally, leftover waste residual carbon was reused for adsorptive removal of hazardous dyes. The developed N-CDs based sensing technique successfully applied for sensing of metal ions and nitro compound from local water bodies with good recovery rate. The present study shows that waste okra peels derived N-CDs can act as a good fluorescent probe for sensing of heavy metal ions and nitro compounds with low limit of detection and have potential use for environmental remediation.
